hero.headline: Ophthalmology workflow, not a generic EHR with an eye-care skin.
hero.subhead: ChartNav is built for the way an ophthalmology day actually runs — patient intake, technician workup, imaging context, physician note, sign-off, and operational visibility — with AI-assisted documentation supporting the physician at every step.

encounterWorkspace.headline: One screen for the whole visit. Not five.
encounterWorkspace.lead: ChartNav keeps the visit context — intake, technician workup, imaging, physician note, sign-off, and the billing signal — on one workspace instead of spread across modules. The physician opens a chart that is already prepared.

cards.title: Intake before check-in.
cards.body: Without ChartNav, the front desk re-types every patient. With it, the intake the patient completed at home becomes the chart your staff queue is already showing.

cards.title: The chart is ready before the physician opens it.
cards.body: Without ChartNav, physicians rebuild the visit. With it, the technician's workup is in the chart before the exam-lane door opens.

cards.title: Imaging in context, not in another app.
cards.body: OCT, fundus, visual-field, and topography route into the same encounter the physician is reading from. No second viewer to alt-tab to.

cards.title: Sign once. Done means done.
cards.body: Structured SOAP draft, attestation, immutability after sign, audit trail on every write. The note is the source of truth for everything that follows.

cards.title: Billing isn't a black box.
cards.body: Once a note is signed, the operations dashboard surfaces it — sign-to-export lag, missing-flag rate, reminder load. Leadership sees what's stuck, what's denied, and what needs action.

aiDocumentation.headline: AI that supports the physician — it does not replace clinical judgment.
aiDocumentation.lead: ChartNav's documentation layer is AI-assisted. The product captures dictation, drafts a structured SOAP note, surfaces missing-data flags, and offers coding-prompt suggestions. Every suggestion is reviewable, every action is logged, and every claim of authorship is honest.

items.title: Hands-free scribing during the exam
items.body: Dictate while you examine; ChartNav captures the transcript and structures it into a SOAP draft for review.

items.title: Specialty-aware drafting prompts
items.body: Drafting prompts respect the specialty template chosen for the visit (retina, glaucoma, cataract, comprehensive).

items.title: Missing-flag review
items.body: The note is annotated with flags for fields the provider should verify before sign — not auto-filled past unverifiable findings.

items.title: Coding-prompt suggestions
items.body: Coding suggestions surface as workflow assistance for E/M, eye-codes, and common CPT ranges. They never overwrite the coder or biller's decision.

items.title: Consult-letter generation
items.body: Once a note is signed, a referring-provider letter can be rendered from the encounter record without re-typing into a separate letter tool.

aiDocumentation.disclaimer: ChartNav's AI features support clinicians and staff — they do not make final clinical, coding, or billing decisions. All output is reviewable by qualified personnel before any downstream action.

specialtyTemplates.headline: Templates that respect how the subspecialty actually runs.
specialtyTemplates.lead: Encounter templates are picked at the start of the visit so the workspace shows the right fields, the right imaging slots, and the right drafting prompts. The list grows with the practices we run assessments with.

templates.label: Comprehensive eye exam
templates.body: General ophthalmology adult and pediatric visits, with refraction context and dilation-aware scheduling.

templates.label: Retina
templates.body: Medical and surgical retina with OCT macula, fundus, and FA workflow context. Injection workflow supported as a separate template.

templates.label: Glaucoma
templates.body: IOP trending, visual-field monitoring, OCT RNFL, and disc photo context for longitudinal disease management.

templates.label: Cataract — pre-op and post-op
templates.body: Pre-operative biometry context and post-op day 1 / week 1 / month 1 visit cadence.

templates.label: Post-operative follow-up
templates.body: Generic post-op template for surgical workflows when a sub-specialty template is not the right fit.

templates.label: Intravitreal injection
templates.body: Procedure-focused template with timing, eye-of-injection, and drug-of-record fields.

templates.label: Visual-field monitoring
templates.body: Field-test visit template designed to surface change vs. baseline without forcing the physician to leave the workspace.

templates.label: Diabetic eye exam
templates.body: Annual diabetic eye exam template with retinopathy grading prompts and PCP-facing letter context.

specialtyTemplates.note: Templates are operator-controlled. ChartNav does not silently auto-promote unverified data into the final note.

codingBilling.headline: Workflow assistance for coding — final authority stays with your staff.
codingBilling.lead: ChartNav surfaces structured fields that support common coding decisions and produces a clean handoff bundle for downstream PM/RCM systems. Final coding and billing decisions remain with qualified coders and billers in the practice.

items.title: E/M and eye-code prompt support
items.body: Visit context surfaces relevant E/M and eye-code ranges as prompts. The coder confirms; ChartNav records what was selected.

items.title: CPT and ICD-10 hand-off
items.body: Structured fields export into a documented JSON / CSV / PDF bundle (the v1.0 canonical handoff payload) for the PM / RCM system the practice already uses.

items.title: Sign-to-export lag tracking
items.body: The operations dashboard reports the median time from sign to export so leadership can see where the bundle is stalling.

items.title: No silent claim submission
items.body: ChartNav does not transmit claims, does not submit to payers, and is not a clearinghouse. Every export is a deliberate operator action.

codingBilling.disclaimer: Coding suggestions are workflow assistance, not coding authority. Practices remain responsible for the accuracy of submitted claims.

imaging.headline: Imaging routed to the chart, not a parallel app.
imaging.lead: ChartNav is designed to support ophthalmic imaging workflow and future device integrations. Today the product routes uploaded studies into the right encounter context and surfaces them in the physician's workspace.

items.title: OCT, fundus, visual field, topography
items.body: The four imaging surfaces ophthalmology actually uses, treated as first-class encounter artifacts.

items.title: Encounter context preserved
items.body: Studies stay attached to the visit they were captured for, surfaced with the note, and visible to the physician on the same workspace.

items.title: Imaging review queue
items.body: An operational queue for unread studies so the practice can see where reads are pending without opening a separate viewer.

imaging.futurePathways: Direct device integration (DICOM, vendor SDKs, modality-worklist routing) is assessment-dependent and lands per pilot scope. ChartNav does not claim certified DICOM connectivity unless and until a specific integration ships.

qualityReadiness.headline: Designed to capture structured data that can support quality reporting workflows.
qualityReadiness.lead: ChartNav captures the structured fields that quality programs typically need: visit type, diagnosis codes, intervention timing, follow-up cadence, and physician sign attribution. As the product matures, these structured signals become the substrate for value-based-care reporting workflows the practice runs.

items.title: Structured visit and diagnosis capture
items.body: Encounter templates produce structured outputs the practice can map to its own quality reporting workflows.

items.title: Audit trail on every write
items.body: An append-oriented audit log captures every meaningful write so post-hoc review is possible without reverse-engineering free text.

items.title: Sign + attestation discipline
items.body: Notes carry an explicit attestation and an immutability gate after sign, which programs that require attribution typically rely on.

qualityReadiness.disclaimer: ChartNav is not a certified MIPS or quality-program reporting tool today. Quality / value-based-care reporting is workflow support that the practice extends with its own reporting stack and qualified personnel.

tabletFlow.headline: Built for the lane, not just the desk.
tabletFlow.lead: Ophthalmology runs on lanes, not desktops. ChartNav's surfaces are designed for iPad Safari with safe-area awareness, larger touch targets, numeric-keypad inputs for IOP and VA, and sticky action rails that clear the home indicator. The technician, the physician, and the front desk each get a workspace tuned to what they're actually doing in that moment.

items.title: Lane-mobile physician review
items.body: The encounter workspace is usable on iPad Pro 12.9 in both orientations, designed against the documented Phase A tablet hardening spec.

items.title: Front desk on tablet or desktop
items.body: Intake queue and reminder CRUD work the same on a counter desktop and on the front-desk tablet.

items.title: Technician quick-entry inputs
items.body: Specialized inputs for IOP (decimal keypad), VA (slash form), and MRN (no auto-capitalize) so workup data lands clean.

tabletFlow.limitation: ChartNav today is a web application served to iPad Safari. There is no native iOS app, no Apple Watch surface, and no offline-first documentation layer.

interoperability.headline: Integration pathways, scoped honestly per pilot.
interoperability.lead: ChartNav is designed to coexist with the EHR, PM, billing, and imaging systems an ophthalmology practice already runs. Integration depth is decided during the workflow assessment — not promised on a marketing page.

rows.body: Standalone, integrated read-through (FHIR R4 read), and integrated write-through modes. Vendor adapters are scoped per pilot.
FHIR R4 read for Patient and Encounter resources. A canonical v1.0 JSON handoff payload is the documented integration shape.
Imaging is routed via upload + encounter context today. Direct device integration (DICOM, modality worklist) is assessment-dependent.
X12 837P and FHIR Claim shapes are documented as Phase B sketches. ChartNav does not transmit claims today.
A messaging seam is wired with a stub provider so opt-in / opt-out / state-machine logic is end-to-end testable. Real carrier wiring is Phase C and requires operator BAA + credentials.
Referring-provider directory + consult-letter PDF generated from the signed note. Delivery is download today; email + fax-stub are recorded as intent only.

interoperability.disclaimer: ChartNav is not ONC-certified, is not a HIPAA Business Associate of a messaging vendor by default, and does not represent itself as a clearinghouse. Integration claims are scoped to what is actually wired in the pilot.

proof.headline: What's actually shipping.
proof.lead: We do not publish customer logos or testimonials we have not earned. Here is what is actually true about the product right now.

facts.label: Phase 2 Pilot Threshold complete
facts.body: Referring-provider letters, admin dashboard, digital intake, messaging hardening, post-visit summary, demo environment, and onboarding/training docs — all shipped on the feature/phase-2-pilot-threshold branch.

facts.label: Five product walkthrough clips
facts.body: Recorded against the live local stack: intake-to-queue, technician workup, imaging-in-chart, consult letter, hands-free scribing, and admin operations.

facts.label: Deterministic demo environment
facts.body: A docker-compose / Makefile path that produces a stable demo database with John Doe + Dr. Sarah Patel + 20 encounters, so every demo runs the same screens.

facts.label: Workflow assessment as the entry point
facts.body: Pilot scope, exit criteria, support tier, and 30/60/90 runbook are documented in-repo and signed before any production-credentialed account is provisioned.

summary.headline: Focused ophthalmology workflow with honest scope.

points.title: Ophthalmology-first, not ophthalmology-skinned
points.body: The encounter workspace, templates, imaging routing, and tablet flow are built around eye-care lane work — not a general-purpose EHR with an eye module.

points.title: AI documentation support, not authority
points.body: Hands-free scribing, drafting, missing-flag review, and coding prompts are workflow assistance. Final clinical, coding, and billing decisions stay with qualified personnel.

points.title: Staged rollout, scoped by assessment
points.body: Standalone, integrated read-through, and integrated write-through modes are decided per practice during the workflow assessment. Nothing is promised on the marketing page.

points.title: Operational visibility built in
points.body: Six KPI cards plus a 14-day trend mean leadership can review usage on real numbers — sign-to-export lag, missing-flag resolution rate, reminder overdue count — instead of anecdotes.

points.title: Modern surface, premium-medical-software tone
points.body: iPad-friendly UI, deliberate vertical rhythm, and explicit truth-statements on every advisory surface.

points.title: Transparent scope
points.body: What ships is what ships. What's in roadmap is labeled roadmap. What is documentation-only is labeled documentation-only.

finalCta.headline: Run a workflow assessment for your practice.
finalCta.body: We start every relationship with a scoped assessment — providers, templates, deployment mode, exit criteria, and support tier — before any production-credentialed account is provisioned.
